metadata.subject: History and Political Science
metadata.board: Maharashtra State Board of Textbook Production and Curriculum Research

content_chunks.heading: Tradition of Historiography
content_chunks.text: Historical research, writing, and studies are carried out with an objective of understanding the chronology of the past events and the interconnections of past events. Historical research is a continuous process. In the physical and natural sciences, the empirical method is used to verify the available knowledge. This empirical method allows formulating laws that remain true irrespective of the time and space. In historical research, it may not be possible to use the method of laboratory experiments and observation because historians were not present in the historical time and space and the historical events cannot be recreated. The writing of critical historical narrative is known as Historiography. A scholar who writes such a narrative is a historian. The historian cannot include every past event in the narrative. The inclusion and interpretation of historical events by the historian often depends on the conceptual framework adopted by the historian.

content_chunks.heading: Modern Historiography
content_chunks.text: Modern historiography has four main characteristics. First, the method of modern historiography is based on scientific principles and begins with the formation of relevant questions. Second, these questions are anthropocentric, meaning the questions are about the deeds of the members of ancient human societies of a particular period. History does not suggest any interrelation between the Divine and human deeds. Third, answers to these questions are supported by reliable evidence. Fourth, history presents a graph of mankind’s journey with the help of past human deeds. Modern historiography has its roots in ancient Greek historical writings. History is originally a Greek term. Herodotus, the Greek historian of the fifth century B.C.E., used the term history first for the book of Herodotus entitled, 'The Histories'.

content_chunks.heading: Notable Scholars: René Descartes
content_chunks.text: René Descartes was the foremost among scholars who insisted on verifying the reliability of historical documents by critically examining the documents. Among the rules given by René Descartes in the book of René Descartes, 'Discourse on the Method', the following rule is supposed to have a great impact on the scientific method of research: Never to accept anything for true till all grounds of doubt are excluded.

content_chunks.heading: Notable Scholars: Voltaire
content_chunks.text: Voltaire's original name was François-Marie Arouet. Voltaire was French. Voltaire opined that along with objective truth and chronology of historical events, considering social traditions, trade, economy, and agriculture was also equally important in historiography. This opinion gave rise to the thought that understanding all aspects of human life is important for history writing. Thus, it is said that Voltaire was the founder of modern historiography.

content_chunks.heading: Notable Scholars: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel
content_chunks.text: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel was a German philosopher. Hegel insisted that the historical reality should be presented in a logical manner. To Hegel, the timeline of historical events was indicative of progress. Hegel also thought that the presentation of history is bound to change over time as new evidence would come forth. Hegel's method of analysis, which is based on opposites, is known as 'Dialectics'. In this method, a theory is proposed at the beginning, which is called 'Thesis'. Then another theory is proposed, which is contrary to the thesis, called 'Antithesis'. After a thorough logical discussion of both the thesis and the antithesis, a new thesis is proposed which includes the gist of both. This process of arriving at the new thesis is called 'Synthesis'.

content_chunks.heading: Notable Scholars: Leopold von Ranké
content_chunks.text: Leopold von Ranké of Berlin University spoke about the critical method of historical research. Leopold von Ranké put emphasis on the utmost importance of information gathered through original documents. Leopold von Ranké also stated that all types of documents associated with a historical event need to be examined with greatest care. Leopold von Ranké believed that with this method it was possible to reach the historical truth. Leopold von Ranké criticised imaginative narration of history.

content_chunks.heading: Notable Scholars: Karl Marx
content_chunks.text: In the latter half of the nineteenth century, a new school of thought arose based on the thesis formulated by Karl Marx. According to Karl Marx, history was not about abstract ideas; history was about living people. Human relationships are shaped by the fundamental needs of people and the ownership as well as nature of prevalent means of production to meet those needs. The accessibility of these means to different strata of the society may not be equal. This inequality causes a division of the society into classes, leading to class struggle. According to Karl Marx, human history is the history of class struggle, as the class that owns the means of production economically exploits the rest of the classes. 'Das Kapital', a treatise written by Karl Marx, is the most referred book all over the world.

content_chunks.heading: Annales School and Feminist Historiography
content_chunks.text: At the onset of the twentieth century, a new school of historiography arose in France, which is known as 'Annales School'. Annales school gave a new direction to history writing. It was recognized now that history is not only about political events, kings, and wars but also about the climate, local people, agriculture, trade, and technology. Feminist historiography means the restructuring of the history from the perspective of women. The writings of Simone de Beauvoir helped in establishing the fundamentals of feminism. Feminist historiography emphasized not only on the inclusion of women in history but also on the rethinking of the male-dominated perspective of history.

content_chunks.heading: Notable Scholars: Michel Foucault
content_chunks.text: Michel Foucault, the French historian of the twentieth century, brought forth a new concept in historiography. Michel Foucault, in the book of Michel Foucault, 'Archaeology of Knowledge', argued that the prevailing practice of arranging historical events in a chronological order is not right. Michel Foucault drew attention to the fact that archaeology does not strive to reach the ultimate historical truth but attempts to explain various transitions in the past. Michel Foucault felt that explaining the transitions in history is more important. Michel Foucault called this method 'the archaeology of knowledge'.

content_chunks.heading: Tradition of Indian Historiography: Ancient Period
content_chunks.text: In the ancient period in India, memories of the great deeds of ancestors and mythological lore were preserved with the help of oral recitals. The inscriptions on the Harappan seals and other artefacts confirm that Indians had mastered the art of writing as early as the third millennium B.C.E. The earliest known written documents of historical nature found in India are in the form of inscriptions dated to the 3rd century B.C.E., during Emperor Ashok Maurya’s times. The edicts of Ashok Maurya are inscribed on natural rocks and stone pillars. From the 1st century C.E., inscriptions begin to occur on coins, metal images, and copper plates. These inscriptions provide important historical information such as the dates of various kings, dynastic genealogies, and territorial extent.

content_chunks.heading: Historiography in the Medieval Period
content_chunks.text: The style of writing 'Rajtarangini', the history of Kashmir by Kalhana in the 12th century C.E., is quite close to the concept of modern historiography. Kalhana wrote this text after critically examining various sources like inscriptions, coins, and dynastic records. In medieval India, historians in the courts of Muslim rulers were influenced by Arabic and Persian historiography. Ziauddin Barani holds an important place; in 'Tarikh-i-Phiruz Shahi', Ziauddin Barani stated that the historian’s duty is not limited only to the recording of the ruler’s valour but also the ruler's failings. 'Bakhar' is an important type of historical document of medieval times containing eulogies of heroes. 'Sabhasad Bakhar' was written by Krishnaji Anant Sabhasad during the reign of Chhatrapati Rajaram Maharaj.

content_chunks.heading: Indian Historiography: Modern Period and Colonial Framework
content_chunks.text: In the 20th century, Indian archaeological exploration started under British rule. Sir Alexander Cunningham, the first Director General of the Archaeological Survey of India, chose sites mentioned in Buddhist texts for excavation. Harappan Civilisation was discovered during the tenure of Sir John Marshall. Many British officials wrote about Indian history, reflecting the colonial policies of the British. James Mill wrote 'The History of British India', published in 1817, which was the first book on Indian history written by a British historian. Colonial historiography often ridiculed Indian culture to justify British rule.

content_chunks.heading: Orientalist and Nationalistic Historiography
content_chunks.text: European scholars who felt curious about civilisations of the East were known as 'Orientalists'. Sir William Jones founded the Asiatic Society in Kolkata in 1784. Friedrich Max Muller translated the Sanskrit text of 'Hitopadesh' into German and edited 50 volumes of 'The Sacred Books of the East'. Nationalistic historiography in India was practiced by historians trained in the British educational system who aimed to restore pride in India's ancient glory. Vishnushastri Chiplunkar inspired nationalistic writings in Maharashtra. Vinayak Damodar Savarkar wrote 'The Indian War of Independence, 1857', which is of great importance to nationalistic historiography.

content_chunks.heading: Historiography in the Post-Independence Era
content_chunks.text: In the post-independence era, Indian scholars began to feel the need of writing histories of various communities, sciences, economic systems, and political ideologies. The historiography of the post-independence era has been influenced mainly by three ideological schools: Marxist History, Subaltern History, and Feminist History. Marxist historians in India focused on the means of production, modes of production, and industrial relations. Notable Indian Marxist historians include Damodar Dharmanand Kosambi, Comrade Shripad Amrut Dange, Ram Sharan Sharma, and Comrade Sharad Patil. Comrade Dange was a founder member of the Indian Communist Party and wrote 'Primitive Communism to Slavery'.

content_chunks.heading: Subaltern History
content_chunks.text: The seeds of subaltern history are found in Marxist historiography. The Italian historian Antonio Gramsci developed the idea that history should be written starting from the bottommost ranks of people in society. Ranjit Guha, an Indian historian, played a major role in establishing subaltern history as an academic school. In India, Mahatma Jyotirao Phule and Dr. Babasaheb Ambedkar expressed similar thoughts earlier. Mahatma Phule unfolded the history of the 'shudratishudra' communities in the book 'Gulamgiri', drawing attention to the exploitation of women and shudras under the name of religion. Dr. Ambedkar consistently wrote about the role of Dalit castes, with 'Who Were the Shudras' and 'The Untouchables' serving as examples of subaltern history.

content_chunks.heading: Feminist Historiography in India
content_chunks.text: Feminist historiography involves restructuring history from the perspective of women. For a long time, male scholars were primarily involved in writing Indian history, leaving the role of women neglected. Tarabai Shinde was the foremost woman author in the 19th century; Tarabai Shinde wrote 'Stripurush Tulana' in 1882, which is acknowledged as the first feminist book in India. Pandita Ramabai published 'The High Caste Hindu Woman' in 1888. In the post-independence era, feminist literature focused on employment, political equality, and treatment at the workplace. Meera Kosambi wrote 'Crossing Thresholds: Feminist Essays in Social History'. Dr. Sharmila Rege's work 'Writing Caste, Writing Gender: Reading Dalit Women’s Testimonies' is noteworthy for including essays on the autobiographies of Dalit women.

content_chunks.heading: What is Applied History?
content_chunks.text: Applied History is also known as 'Public History'. Applied History is a field of study concerned with the application of history for the benefit of people in contemporary and future times. Knowledge of history is useful as history can provide guidance in finding solutions to contemporary social issues and incorporate historical knowledge into social planning. Public History helps to overcome misgivings that history is only for historians and has no applicability to practical life. Many universities abroad offer courses in Public History. In India, the Srushti Institute of Art Design and Technology in Bengaluru has an independent department named 'Centre for Public History'.

content_chunks.heading: Applied History and Research in Various Fields: Philosophy and Science
content_chunks.text: The history of philosophy helps in understanding the origin of various ideologies and the intellectual traditions giving rise to those ideologies. Philosophy needs language as a medium of expression, so the history of language proves useful. The history of science helps in understanding the chronological order of scientific discoveries, inventions, and theories. History of science can also help to understand the cause-effect chain that led to discoveries. Scientific discoveries are often the effects of human efforts to fulfill a need and curiosity, based on already available scientific knowledge.

content_chunks.heading: Applied History and Research in Various Fields: Technology and Industry
content_chunks.text: The history of technology helps in understanding changes and the causes of changes in fields like agricultural production, architecture, and engineering. Scientific discoveries and technological advancement are mutually dependent. Knowledge of science and technology was important from the making of stone tools to the mechanisation of production. The field of industry and commerce expands with the growth of trade, promoting the development of cultural interactions. It is essential to understand the history of these processes as the nature of market and commerce continues to change.

content_chunks.heading: Applied History and Research in Various Fields: Management and Arts
content_chunks.text: In management studies, it is essential to have knowledge of functional systems of the past to understand factors involved in the chain of production, human resources, and sales management. Knowledge of history makes management at various levels easier. Regarding arts, it is important to understand the development of various art forms through the style of expression and intellectual-emotional-cultural traditions. The key to expressions in any art form and the emotional temperament of the artist can be understood with the help of cultural history.

content_chunks.heading: Management of Cultural and Natural Heritage
content_chunks.text: Cultural Heritage is in the form of human creation and is divided into tangible and intangible types. Tangible Cultural Heritage includes ancient sites, buildings, artefacts, manuscripts, and paintings. Intangible Cultural Heritage includes oral traditions, traditional knowledge, social customs, rituals, and performing arts. Natural Heritage gives importance to biodiversity, including fauna, flora, and ecology. UNESCO, a global organisation, has announced directives to promote the preservation of cultural and natural heritage. On the basis of these directives, sites and traditions are declared as 'World Heritage'. The Western Ghats and the Kaas plateau in Maharashtra are included in the list of UNESCO’s World Natural Heritage.

content_chunks.heading: History of Indian Arts: Visual Arts
content_chunks.text: Art is acknowledged when a natural human instinct to share experience and wisdom results in a beautiful creation. Artistic creations are of two types: Visual Arts (Drik Kala) and Performing Arts (Lalit or Aangik Kala). Folk art is a tradition continued from prehistoric times, created by collective participation. Classical art is expressed within an established frame of consistent rules and requires prolonged training. Maratha style of painting began to develop in the latter half of the 17th century C.E. and consists of coloured paintings occurring as murals and miniatures. Murals of Maratha style can be seen in old wadas at places like Wai, Menavali, and Satara in Maharashtra.

content_chunks.heading: Folk and Classical Styles of Painting
content_chunks.text: Rock paintings dating to stone ages have been discovered in states like Madhya Pradesh and Uttar Pradesh. The rock paintings in the caves at Bhimbetka are a World Heritage site. The traditions of Warli painting and Pingul or Chitrakathi in Maharashtra are fine examples of folk style. Jivya Somya Mashe played a great role in making Warli painting popular. Classical styles of painting are mentioned in ancient Indian texts as 'alekhyam'. Classical painting is said to have six main aspects (Shadange): Roopbheda (shapes), Pramana (proportion), Bhava (expressions), Lavanyayojana (aesthetics), Sadrushyata (resemblance to reality), and Varnikabhang (colour composition).

content_chunks.heading: Miniature and Western Styles of Painting
content_chunks.text: Miniature paintings in early manuscripts show Persian influence. The Mughal miniature style developed during the reign of Emperor Akbar as a blend of Indian and Persian styles. In the British period, Indian artists came under the influence of European styles. An art school was established under James Wales, a Scottish artist, in Pune. Gangaram Tambat, a Marathi artist who worked with James Wales, made drawings of the rock-cut caves at Verul and Karle. A number of renowned artists were trained in the J. J. School of Art and Industry, established in 1857 C.E.

content_chunks.heading: Sculptural Art and Iconography
content_chunks.text: Sculptures are three-dimensional images or statues made from rock, metal, or clay. The temple of Kailasa at Verul is a unique monolithic sculpture carved from a single rock. The lion capital of the Ashokan pillar at Sarnath is the national emblem of India. The Gandhara school of art arose in the 2nd century B.C.E. in Afghanistan with Greek and Persian influence. The Mathura school of art rose during the Kushana period and laid the foundation of Indian iconography. The art of making bronze images was developed under the patronage of Chola kings between the 9th and 13th centuries, creating idols like Nataraja.

content_chunks.heading: Indian Architecture: Caves and Temples
content_chunks.text: Rock-cut caves in India originated in the 3rd century B.C.E. and represent a union of architecture and sculptural art. Temple architecture began to develop around the 4th century C.E. during the Gupta period. The Nagara style of North India and the Dravida style of South India are the two principal styles of Indian temple architecture. A blend of these two is known as Vesara style. Hemadpanti temples in Maharashtra, built in the 12th-13th centuries, are known for star-shaped plans and zigzag designs on outer walls. Hemadpanti walls are built without mortar, using tenon and mortise joints to lock stones.

content_chunks.heading: Islamic and Indo-Gothic Architecture
content_chunks.text: In the medieval period, Islamic architecture in India blended Persian, Central Asian, and Arabic styles. The Kutub Minar, Taj Mahal, and Gol Gumbaz are world-famous examples. The Kutub Minar is 73 meters high. The Taj Mahal was built by Emperor Shah Jahan. The Gol Gumbaz at Bijapur houses the burial of Mohammed Adil Shah and features a whispering gallery. During the British period, the Indo-Gothic architectural style arose, used for churches and government offices. The Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Railway Terminus in Mumbai is a fine example of Indo-Gothic architecture and is a World Heritage Site.

content_chunks.heading: Indian Traditions of Performing Arts
content_chunks.text: India has independent and varied traditions of folk songs, folk instrumental music, folk dances, and folk theatre. In Maharashtra, performing folk arts include Koli Dance, Tarapa Dance, Dashavatar of Konkan, Powada, Keertan, and Jagar-Gondhal. India also has a rich heritage of Classical performing arts. The text of 'Natyashastra' written by Bharatmuni is the earliest text discussing music and theatre. The nine moods or nine rasas are fundamental in the presentations of Indian performing arts: Shringar (love), Hasya (humour), Beebhatsa (repulsion), Raudra (terrible), Karuna (sad), Veer (heroic), Bhayanak (fearful), Adbhut (wondrous), and Shant (peaceful). Indian classical music has two main branches: Hindustani music and Carnatic music.

content_chunks.heading: Art, Applied Art and Professional Opportunities
content_chunks.text: Art history is an independent branch of knowledge with research opportunities. Art historians can work in journalism or the art market, which requires special expertise to assess the value of art objects. Applied art refers to artistic creations with a utilitarian purpose or utility value to make the creations economically viable. Fields of applied arts include industry and advertisement, interior design, production of ornamental objects, stage backdrops, and art direction for films. The National Institute of Design in Ahmedabad, Gujarat, is a world-renowned institute offering technical and occupational training in these fields.

content_chunks.heading: Introduction and History of Mass Media
content_chunks.text: Mass Media includes various modes of communication used to send information to far-away destinations. Newspapers are publications that print news, editorials, and people's opinions, serving as historical documents that record current events. Before the common era, Egypt had a custom of placing inscriptions with royal decrees in public places. In the Roman Empire, royal decrees known as 'Acta Diurna' (acts of everyday) were placed in public places. The first English newspaper of India, 'Bengal Gazette', was printed on 29th January 1780 and was started by James Augustus Hickey.

content_chunks.heading: Significant Indian Newspapers
content_chunks.text: The newspaper 'Darpan' was started in 1832 in Mumbai with Balshastri Jambhekar as the editor. 'Darpan' reviewed political, economic, and social events. 'Prabhakar' was started by Bhau Mahajan and published the history of the French revolution. 'Dnyanoday' printed the map of Asia in 1842 and the map of Europe in 1851, and was the first Indian newspaper to print an illustration. 'Kesari' and 'Maratha' were started by Gopal Ganesh Agarkar and Bal Gangadhar Tilak in 1881 to speak about social and political issues before independence.

content_chunks.heading: Radio and Television in India
content_chunks.text: The Indian Broadcasting Company (IBC) was a private radio company that was later taken over by the British Government and named 'Indian State Broadcasting Service' (ISBS). On 8th June 1936, the service was renamed 'All India Radio' (AIR). After independence, All India Radio became an integral part of the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting and was named 'Akashvani'. Television started in India with the inauguration of the Delhi Doordarshan Centre by Dr. Rajendra Prasad. Doordarshan's Mumbai centre began telecasting on 1st May 1972. 'Bharat Ek Khoj', a serial directed by Shyam Benegal and based on Pandit Nehru's 'Discovery of India', presented the history of India from ancient to modern periods.

content_chunks.heading: Entertainment and History: Folk Theatre
content_chunks.text: Entertainment can be classified into active and passive categories. Active entertainment requires an individual's mental-physical participation, while passive entertainment involves being a viewer. Puppetry or 'Kathputali' is a traditional art with remains found in Harappan sites. Dashavatara Theatre is part of the folk theatre in Maharashtra, presented in Konkan and Goa after the harvesting season, based on the 10 incarnations of Vishnu. Bhajan involves singing devotional songs in praise of God, accompanied by instruments like cymbals and mridangam. There are two types of Bhajan: Chakri Bhajan and Songi Bhajan.

content_chunks.heading: Keertan, Lalit, and Tamasha
content_chunks.text: Keertan tradition is said to have been founded by Naradmuni, with Saint Namadev being the first keertankar of Maharashtra. Keertan is performed in a temple and requires the keertankar to be well-informed in oratory, singing, and dance. Lalit is an old form of entertainment in Maharashtra belonging to the Naradiya Keertan tradition. Bharud is a metaphorical song with spiritual and ethical lessons, made popular by Saint Eknath. Tamasha is a Persian word meaning 'pleasing sight' and developed as an independent art form in the 18th century by absorbing traits of folk theatre and classical arts.

content_chunks.heading: Marathi Theatre and Indian Film Industry
content_chunks.text: Vishnudas Bhave is known as the father of Marathi theatre, and 'Seetaswayamvar' was the first play presented by Vishnudas Bhave. Initially, Marathi plays had no written scripts, but V.J. Kirtane wrote the first script for 'Thorale Madhavrao Peshwe' in 1861. The Indian film industry began with the technology of motion pictures. The first full-length movie, 'Raja Harischandra', was directed by Dadasaheb Phalke and released in 1913. Maharashtra is known as the land that nurtured the Indian film industry. Historical movies like 'Mughal-e-Azam' and 'Sant Tukaram' have received international acclaim.

content_chunks.heading: Sports and History: Importance and Types
content_chunks.text: Sports combine entertainment and physical exercise. The history of sports begins with the origin of mankind. Ancient Indian literature mentions games like dice, wrestling, and chariot races. The Greeks were the first to standardise the rules of sports and organise the Olympics. Sports are divided into Indoor Games (played in a closed environment like chess and carrom) and Outdoor Games (like Kabaddi, Kho-kho, Cricket, and Football). Major Dhyan Chand was an expert hockey player and captain of the Indian team that won a Gold Medal in the 1936 Berlin Olympics. 29th August, the birth date of Dhyan Chand, is celebrated as National Sports Day in India.

content_chunks.heading: Globalisation of Sports and Professional Opportunities
content_chunks.text: The field of sports in the 20th-21st century was influenced by globalisation, allowing international matches to be watched on television worldwide. This has significantly affected the sports economy as industrial companies use matches to advertise. Professional opportunities in sports include sports journalism, where writers create articles and reviews about events like the Olympics. Expert commentators are in demand and must have knowledge of history, statistics, and previous records of the games. Toys also throw light on history; for example, an ivory doll found in Pompeii dated to the 1st century C.E. indicates Indo-Roman trade relations.

content_chunks.heading: Tourism in the Past and Types of Tourism
content_chunks.text: Tourism is defined as travelling to visit places in distant regions for a specific purpose. In the past, people travelled for pilgrimage, trade, and education. Buddhist literature mentions that Gautam Buddha travelled to several cities for preaching. In the 19th century, Thomas Cook organised a trip by railway for 600 people, opening doors for modern tourism. Tourism is typified into several categories. Local and Interstate tourism occurs within one's own country. International tourism has become easier due to aviation and economic liberalisation. Historical tourism satisfies interest in history, such as visiting forts in Maharashtra. Geographic tourism involves visiting natural wonders like the crater lake at Lonar. Health tourism attracts foreigners to India for cheaper medical facilities and Yoga. Agro-tourism is meant for the urban population to experience rural life. Sports tourism involves attending events like the Olympics or Maharashtra Kesari.

content_chunks.heading: Conservation of Heritage and Professional Opportunities
content_chunks.text: Heritage is divided into Natural Heritage and Cultural Heritage. Preservation of heritage sites is essential for future generations. Heritage Walks are organised group activities for visiting historical places, popular in cities like Ahmedabad, Mumbai, and Pune. Tourism and Hospitality is an industry with potential for maximum employment. The Maharashtra Tourism Development Corporation (MTDC) was set up in 1975 to develop tourism in Maharashtra. Bhilar, a village near Mahabaleshwar, is known as the 'Village of Books' where every household maintains a library for tourists. Cultural tourism includes visiting educational institutes and local festivals to appreciate the achievements of local people.

content_chunks.heading: Heritage Management: Sources and Training
content_chunks.text: Heritage management involves the conservation and preservation of historical sources. Oral sources of history include folk songs and folk tales, requiring training in sociology and linguistics. Written sources include official records, diaries, and manuscripts, requiring knowledge of scripts like Brahmi and Modi. Material sources include artefacts and fossils, requiring knowledge of archaeological methods. Only trained persons can take up the tasks of collecting, classifying, and preserving these sources. Libraries, museums, and archives are the primary institutes that conserve and manage the precious historical heritage.

content_chunks.heading: Famous Museums and Encyclopaedias
content_chunks.text: The Louvre Museum in France was established in the 18th century and houses the 'Monalisa' by Leonardo da Vinci. The British Museum in London houses about 80 lakh objects. In India, the Indian Museum in Kolkata was the first museum, established in 1814. The Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Vastusangrahalay in Mumbai was built in Indo-Gothic style. The Saraswati Mahal Library in Thanjavur was built in the 16th-17th century. Encyclopaedias are systematic compilations of knowledge. Types of encyclopaedias include Dictionaries, Comprehensive Encyclopaedias (Vishwakosh), Encyclopaedic Literature (Koshsadrush), and Indexes. Encyclopaedias make vast volumes of knowledge accessible and are indicators of the cultural ripeness of a society.

content_chunks.heading: Working of the Indian Constitution
content_chunks.text: The Indian Constitution established a secular, democratic Republic to ensure justice and freedom for citizens. Democracy requires political maturity, where principles of democracy are integrated into actual practice. The Indian Constitution guaranteed adult suffrage, and the voting age was reduced from 21 to 18 years. Decentralisation of power is central to democratic government, empowered by the 73rd and 74th amendments. Social justice and equality are objectives aimed at ending discrimination based on caste, religion, or gender. The Scheduled Castes and Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act prevents injustice. The Judiciary protects the basic framework of the Constitution and ensures that the Parliament cannot alter the basic structure of the Constitution.

content_chunks.heading: The Electoral Process in India
content_chunks.text: The Election Commission of India administers the election process. Article 324 of the Indian Constitution established this autonomous body. Sukumar Sen was the first Chief Election Commissioner. The functions of the Election Commission include preparing voters lists, deciding the timetable, and giving recognition to political parties. The Code of Conduct explains rules to be followed by the government and political parties to ensure free and fair elections. The journey from ballot boxes to Electronic Voting Machines (EVM) started in the 1990s. EVMs include the 'None of the Above' (NOTA) option and the Voter Verified Paper Audit Trail (VVPAT) to stop malpractices.

content_chunks.heading: Political Parties in India
content_chunks.text: Political parties are groups that aim to contest elections and establish government. Characteristics of political parties include having an ideology, a party agenda, and acting as a link between the government and the people. The nature of the party system in India changed from a single dominant party (Congress) to a coalition government system after 1989. National parties include the Indian National Congress, Bharatiya Janata Party, and Communist Party of India. Regional parties focus on regional development and linguistic identity. Examples of regional parties include Shiv Sena in Maharashtra, Shiromani Akali Dal in Punjab, and Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam (DMK) in Tamil Nadu.

content_chunks.heading: Social and Political Movements
content_chunks.text: Movements are organised collective actions focused on particular social issues. Tribal movements in India, led by figures like Birsa Munda, demanded rights over forest land. Farmers movements arose due to anti-agriculture policies and gained momentum after the Green Revolution. The labour movement emerged with industrialisation, leading to the formation of the All India Trade Union Congress in 1920. The women's movement aimed at eliminating injustice and was supported by reformists like Savitribai Phule and Tarabai Shinde. Environment movements work on protecting biodiversity and water sources. The consumer movement emerged after the Consumer Protection Act of 1986 to protect citizens from frauds like adulteration.

content_chunks.heading: Challenges faced by Indian Democracy
content_chunks.text: Democracy is a continuous living process that faces several threats. Communalism and terrorism hamper social stability and reduce people's participation. Naxalism began as a movement for landless farmers but has become a violent struggle. Corruption in the public sector reduces the government's working capacity and people's confidence in the democratic process. The criminalisation of politics increases the role of money and muscle power. To make democracy successful, majority opinion must include the interests of minorities. The Judiciary makes conscious efforts to make the political process transparent. Increasing the participation of people at all levels is essential for the success of Indian democracy.
